---
title: "Chapter 2: Qualitative Causal Models"
subtitle: "Inference and Intervention | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Identify the **four types of nodes** in a causal model and when each is used
- Assign **signed directed links** ([+]/[-]) to represent qualitative causal relationships
- Construct **conditional certainty tables** for non-monotonic relationships
- Recognize the three fundamental **triplet structures**: serial (chain), diverging (fork), converging (collider)
- Apply **d-separation** criteria to determine when information flows between variables
- Build a complete **qualitative DAG** for an MNH program's investment model
:::


# From Intuition to Structure {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Why writing down causal beliefs matters</span>


## The Problem with Mental Models

Every manager carries **implicit causal beliefs** about how the world works.

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .red}
**Implicit Models**

- Vague and inconsistent
- Resistant to scrutiny
- Hidden assumptions
- Two people can disagree without realizing *why*
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Explicit Models (DAGs)**

- Precise and shareable
- Open to critique and testing
- Assumptions are visible
- Disagreements become *productive*
:::
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Core Principle:** A qualitative causal model is a disciplined way of writing down what you believe about how variables in a system relate to one another — before you add any numbers.
:::


## From Whiteboard Sketch to Formal DAG

The process of building a qualitative model:

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">Identify<br>Variables</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">Classify<br>Node Types</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;">Draw<br>Arrows</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #F8A623;">Assign<br>Signs</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">Validate<br>Structure</div>
</div>

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[MNH Example]{.example-label}

A program director might say: "I think training midwives improves quality of care, and better quality reduces mortality." This becomes the chain: **Training → Quality → Mortality** with signs [+] and [-].
:::

. . .

The rest of this chapter teaches you the formal vocabulary and rules for each step.


# Nodes {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">The four building blocks of causal diagrams</span>


## Node Type 1: Probabilistic Nodes (Ovals)

::: {.definition-box}
**Probabilistic Node (Oval):** A variable whose state is uncertain and determined by nature or context — not by any agent's deliberate choice. Represented as an **oval** (or circle) in the diagram.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[MNH Examples]{.example-label}

- **Neonatal Mortality Rate (NMR):** States = {High (>25/1000), Low (<=25/1000)}
- **Baseline Disease Burden:** States = {Severe, Moderate, Low}
- **Workforce Quality:** States = {Competent, Developing, Inadequate}
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 60%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.6em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">NMR<br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; font-weight: 400;">{High, Low}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">Workforce<br>Quality<br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; font-weight: 400;">{Competent, Developing, Inadequate}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">Disease<br>Burden<br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; font-weight: 400;">{Severe, Moderate, Low}</span></div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

::: {.callout-box}
**Key rule:** Each probabilistic node must have **MECE states** — Mutually Exclusive and Collectively Exhaustive. The variable must be in exactly one state at any given time.
:::


## Node Type 2: Objective Nodes (Hexagons)

::: {.definition-box}
**Objective Node (Hexagon):** A variable representing a goal or performance metric that a decision-maker is trying to optimize. Represented as a **hexagon**. Objective nodes are always leaf nodes — nothing flows out of them.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[MNH Examples]{.example-label}

- **Lives Saved:** The program's ultimate objective — saving hundreds of thousands of lives over the program period
- **Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR):** Measured in deaths per 100,000 live births
- **Cost-Effectiveness:** Lives saved per dollar invested
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 55%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.6em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node hexagon outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 1em; border-style: double;">Lives<br>Saved</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node hexagon outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 1em; border-style: double;">MMR</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node hexagon outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 1em; border-style: double;">Cost-<br>Effectiveness</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

::: {.callout-box}
Objective nodes sit at the end of causal chains. They receive arrows but never send them. They answer: **What are we ultimately trying to affect?**
:::


## Node Type 3: Strategic Option Nodes (Rectangles)

::: {.definition-box}
**Strategic Option Node (Rectangle):** A variable representing a deliberate choice made by a decision-maker. The states are the available options. Represented as a **rectangle**.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[MNH Examples]{.example-label}

- **Budget Allocation:** States = {People-heavy, Products-heavy, Systems-heavy, Balanced}
- **Training Investment:** States = {Fund, Don't Fund}
- **Equipment Procurement:** States = {CPAP, PPH Bundle, Ultrasound, Combination}
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 70%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.6em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">Budget<br>Allocation<br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; font-weight: 400;">{People, Products, Systems, Balanced}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">Training<br>Investment<br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; font-weight: 400;">{Fund, Don't Fund}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">Equipment<br>Procurement<br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; font-weight: 400;">{CPAP, PPH, Ultrasound, Combo}</span></div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

::: {.callout-box}
Strategic option nodes are the **levers** that the MNH program controls. These are root nodes — no causal arrows point into them (except informational links).
:::


## Node Type 4: Function Nodes (Chevrons)

::: {.definition-box}
**Function Node (Chevron):** A variable whose state is **deterministically determined** by its parents — there is no residual uncertainty. Represented as a **chevron** (or pointed rectangle). If you know all parent states, you know this node's state with certainty.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[MNH Examples]{.example-label}

- **Total Budget Spent** = Workforce spending + Equipment spending + Systems spending (a simple sum)
- **Coverage Rate** = Facilities equipped / Total facilities (a deterministic ratio)
:::

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
**Probabilistic Node**

Parent states → probability distribution over child states (uncertainty remains)
:::
::: {.col}
**Function Node**

Parent states → exactly one child state (no uncertainty)
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
Function nodes are rare in qualitative models but become important in quantitative models (Ch 4) where they represent accounting identities, formulas, or physical laws.
:::


# Directed Links {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Arrows, signs, and the language of causation</span>


## Causal Links with Signs

::: {.definition-box}
**Directed Link (Arrow):** An arrow from node A to node B means that A is a **direct cause** of B — changing A can change B (holding all other parents of B fixed). The sign **[+]** or **[-]** indicates the direction of effect.
:::

. . .

| Sign | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **[+]** | Increasing A increases B (or being in "good" state of A makes "good" state of B more likely) | Training [+] → Quality |
| **[-]** | Increasing A decreases B (or being in "good" state of A makes "bad" state of B less likely) | Quality [-] → Mortality |
| **[0]** | A has no effect on B in this combination (used in conditional certainty tables) | — |

. . .

<table style="width: 70%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.6em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Training<br>Investment</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 1.8em;">→ [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node">Workforce<br>Quality</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<span class="dag-arrow negative" style="font-size: 1.8em;">→ [-]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome">Neonatal<br>Mortality</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>


## Parent, Child, and Directed Paths

::: {.definition-box}
**Parent** — a node at the tail of an arrow. **Child** — a node at the head. A **directed path** is a sequence of arrows all pointing in the same direction from an ancestor to a descendant.
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 85%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.55em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Budget<br><span style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #666;">(Root)</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">→ [+]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment">Training<br><span style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #666;">(Child of Budget)</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">→ [+]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node">Quality<br><span style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #666;">(Grandchild)</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow negative">→ [-]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome">NMR<br><span style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #666;">(Leaf)</span></div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.incremental}
- **Root nodes** have no parents — they are exogenous (Budget, Baseline Burden)
- **Leaf nodes** have no children — they are terminal outcomes (NMR, MMR)
- The directed path Budget → Training → Quality → NMR has an **overall sign** of [+] x [+] x [-] = **[-]** (more budget leads to lower mortality)
:::


## Sign Multiplication Along Paths

::: {.callout-box}
**Sign Rule:** The overall sign of a directed path is the product of individual link signs, where [+] x [+] = [+], [-] x [-] = [+], and [+] x [-] = [-].
:::

. . .

| Path | Link Signs | Overall Sign | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Budget → Training → Quality → NMR | [+] x [+] x [-] | **[-]** | More budget reduces NMR |
| Budget → Equipment → Quality → NMR | [+] x [+] x [-] | **[-]** | Same direction |
| Disease Burden → Coverage → NMR | [-] x [-] | **[+]** | Higher burden → higher NMR |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**When multiple paths connect two nodes**, the overall effect is ambiguous if paths have different signs. This is one reason we need quantitative models (Ch 4) — qualitative signs alone cannot resolve conflicting path effects.
:::


## Informational Links into Decision Nodes

::: {.definition-box}
**Informational Link:** A dashed arrow pointing **into** a strategic option node. It represents information available to the decision-maker at the time of the decision — not a causal effect. The decision-maker *knows* the parent's state before choosing.
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 75%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.55em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder">Baseline<br>NMR Data</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<span style="color: #F8A623; font-size: 1.6em;">- - →</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Budget<br>Allocation</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 1.4em;">→ [+]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment">Workforce<br>Deployment</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.incremental}
- The dashed arrow means the program **observes** NMR data before deciding how to allocate the budget
- This is *not* the same as NMR *causing* the budget — it is an information flow
- Informational links matter for **decision analysis** (Ch 7): they determine what you know when you decide
:::


# Conditional Certainty Tables {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">When [+] and [-] are not enough</span>


## When Relationships Are Not Monotonic

A single [+] or [-] sign assumes the relationship is **monotonic** — more of A always means more (or less) of B. But some causal relationships are more complex.

. . .

::: {.definition-box}
**Conditional Certainty Table:** A table showing the qualitative sign ([+], [-], or [0]) of a child variable for each **combination** of parent states. Used when the effect of one parent depends on the state of another parent.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[MNH Example: Effect of CPAP Machines on NMR]{.example-label}

The effect of CPAP depends on whether trained staff are available to operate them:
:::

| | **Staff Trained** | **Staff Not Trained** |
|---|---|---|
| **CPAP Available** | [-] (reduces NMR) | [0] (no effect — machines sit idle) |
| **CPAP Unavailable** | [0] (training alone insufficient) | [0] (no intervention) |


## Reading Conditional Certainty Tables

::: {.callout-box}
**How to read:** Each cell answers the question: "If this parent combination holds, what is the qualitative direction of effect on the child?"
:::

. . .

A richer example — effect on **Quality of Care**:

| | **Equipment Available** | **Equipment Unavailable** |
|---|---|---|
| **Staff Competent** | [+] strong positive | [+] moderate (skill compensates) |
| **Staff Developing** | [+] moderate (equipment helps) | [0] weak or no effect |
| **Staff Inadequate** | [0] equipment cannot compensate | [-] active harm risk |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Key Insight:** Conditional certainty tables reveal **interaction effects** — situations where one input only works when combined with another. For MNH programs, this means **People + Products investments are complements, not substitutes.**
:::


# Serial Triplets (Chain) {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">A → B → C — information flows along the chain</span>


## The Serial Triplet Structure

::: {.definition-box}
**Serial Triplet (Chain):** Three nodes connected as A → B → C. Node B is a **mediator** — it transmits the causal effect of A to C.
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 70%; margin: 0.5em auto; font-size: 0.65em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">A</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 2em;">→</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">B</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 2em;">→</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">C</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

**Information flow rules:**

::: {.incremental}
- **Unconditionally:** Information flows from A to C through B. Learning about A tells you something about C.
- **Conditioning on B blocks the flow.** If you *know* B's state, then learning A tells you nothing new about C.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Intuition:** If I know the mediator's exact value, the upstream cause becomes irrelevant for predicting the downstream effect.
:::


## MNH Example: Training → Competency → Mortality

<table style="width: 80%; margin: 0.4em auto; font-size: 0.6em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">Training<br>Program<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{Completed, Not}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 1.8em;">→ [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">Staff<br>Competency<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{Competent, Developing}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow negative" style="font-size: 1.8em;">→ [-]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">Neonatal<br>Mortality<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{High, Low}</span></div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Not conditioning on Competency:**

Training and Mortality are associated. We can predict lower mortality from knowing training was completed.
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Conditioning on Competency:**

If we *know* a midwife is competent, it no longer matters *how* she became competent (training vs. experience). The path is **blocked**.
:::
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**For the program:** This tells us that training's value is entirely mediated through competency. If we can measure competency directly, we do not need training data to predict mortality.
:::


## Chain Summary

| Condition | A and C associated? | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Not conditioning on B** | Yes | Information flows A → B → C |
| **Conditioning on B** | No | Knowing B blocks the path |

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Think of it as a pipeline]{.example-label}

Water flows from the reservoir (A) through the pipe (B) to the tap (C). If you close the valve at B (condition on it), nothing from A reaches C.
:::


# Diverging Triplets (Fork) {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">A ← B → C — the common cause structure</span>


## The Diverging Triplet Structure

::: {.definition-box}
**Diverging Triplet (Fork):** Three nodes connected as A ← B → C. Node B is a **common cause** of both A and C. This creates a **spurious correlation** between A and C.
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 70%; margin: 0.5em auto; font-size: 0.65em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">A</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 2em;">←</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">B</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 2em;">→</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">C</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

**Information flow rules:**

::: {.incremental}
- **Unconditionally:** A and C appear correlated — but only because B drives both. This is **confounding**.
- **Conditioning on B blocks the flow.** Once you know the common cause, A and C become independent.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Intuition:** Ice cream sales (A) and drowning deaths (C) are correlated — but the common cause is summer heat (B). Knowing the temperature eliminates the spurious link.
:::


## MNH Example: PPH Detection ← Workforce Quality → CPAP Usage

<table style="width: 80%; margin: 0.4em auto; font-size: 0.6em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">PPH<br>Detection Rate<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{High, Low}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 1.8em;">← [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">Workforce<br>Quality<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{Competent, Developing}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 1.8em;">→ [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">Correct CPAP<br>Usage<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{Proper, Improper}</span></div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .red}
**Naive observation:**

Facilities with high PPH detection also have high CPAP usage. Does detecting PPH *cause* better CPAP use?
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Causal reality:**

Both are driven by **workforce quality**. Competent staff detect PPH *and* use CPAP correctly. Controlling for workforce quality eliminates the association.
:::
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**For the program:** If we see a correlation between two program metrics, we must ask — is there a common driver? Investing in PPH protocols will not improve CPAP usage unless we address the underlying workforce quality.
:::


## Fork Summary

| Condition | A and C associated? | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Not conditioning on B** | Yes | Common cause creates spurious correlation |
| **Conditioning on B** | No | Controlling for common cause removes confounding |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**This is exactly the confounding trap from Chapter 1!** The fork structure is the formal representation of confounding. The solution is always: **condition on the common cause**.
:::


# Converging Triplets (Collider) {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">A → B ← C — the dangerous exception</span>


## The Converging Triplet Structure

::: {.definition-box}
**Converging Triplet (Collider):** Three nodes connected as A → B ← C. Node B is a **collider** — two causal arrows "collide" at B. This structure behaves **opposite** to chains and forks.
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 70%; margin: 0.5em auto; font-size: 0.65em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">A</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 2em;">→</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 1em; border-width: 3px;">B</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 2em;">←</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.8em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome" style="padding: 0.5em 1em;">C</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

**Information flow rules (REVERSED from chain and fork!):**

::: {.incremental}
- **Unconditionally:** A and C are **independent**. No information flows — the path is naturally **blocked** at the collider.
- **Conditioning on B (or any descendant of B) OPENS the path.** A and C become associated — this is **collider bias**.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .red}
**Warning:** Conditioning on a collider *creates* a spurious association that did not exist before. This is the opposite of what happens with chains and forks!
:::


## MNH Example: Staffing → Quality of Care ← Equipment

<table style="width: 80%; margin: 0.4em auto; font-size: 0.6em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">Staffing<br>Level<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{Adequate, Low}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 1.8em;">→ [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em; border-width: 3px;">Quality<br>of Care<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{Good, Poor}</span></div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive" style="font-size: 1.8em;">← [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.5em 0.8em;">Equipment<br>Availability<br><span style="font-size: 0.75em; font-weight: 400;">{Available, Unavailable}</span></div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[The "Explaining Away" Effect]{.example-label}

Among facilities with **good** quality of care (conditioning on the collider), staffing and equipment become **negatively** associated: if quality is good *despite* low staffing, we infer equipment must be excellent — and vice versa. Each cause "explains away" the other.
:::

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Not conditioning on Quality:**

Staffing and Equipment are independent (separate investment decisions).
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .red}
**Conditioning on Quality = Good:**

Among good-quality facilities: low staffing → must have good equipment. Creates a spurious *negative* association.
:::
:::
:::


## Collider Summary and the "Explain Away" Intuition

| Condition | A and C associated? | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Not conditioning on B** | No | Path is blocked at the collider |
| **Conditioning on B** | Yes | Collider bias — "explaining away" |

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**The "explain away" intuition:** Imagine you know a student passed an exam (collider). If you then learn the exam was easy, you revise *downward* your belief about the student's talent. The two causes compete to explain the common effect.
:::

. . .

::: {.key-takeaway}
**The Three Triplets — One Critical Table**

| Structure | Unconditional | Conditional on middle |
|---|---|---|
| Chain: A → B → C | Associated | **Blocked** |
| Fork: A ← B → C | Associated | **Blocked** |
| Collider: A → B ← C | **Blocked** | Associated |
:::


# c-Independence and d-Separation {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Formal criteria for when information flows</span>


## From Triplets to Full Graphs

In a simple triplet, the rules are straightforward. But real DAGs have **many paths** between any two nodes, and each path may contain multiple triplet structures.

. . .

::: {.definition-box}
**d-Separation (Directional Separation):** Two nodes X and Y are **d-separated** given a set of conditioning variables Z if **every** path between X and Y is **blocked**. If d-separated, X and Y are conditionally independent given Z.
:::

. . .

A path between X and Y is **blocked** by conditioning set Z if the path contains:

::: {.incremental}
- A **chain** (A → B → C) or **fork** (A ← B → C) where the middle node B **is in** Z, OR
- A **collider** (A → B ← C) where neither B nor any descendant of B **is in** Z
:::


## Applying d-Separation: An Example

Consider this DAG:

<table style="width: 90%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.5em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Budget</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment">Training</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="border-width: 3px;">Quality</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow negative">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome">NMR</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">↑</span></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment">Equipment</div>
</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
**Q: Is Training d-separated from NMR given Quality?**

Path: Training → Quality → NMR

Quality is in the conditioning set and is a **chain** middle node → **Blocked**.

Answer: **Yes**, d-separated.
:::

::: {.col}
**Q: Is Training d-separated from Equipment given Quality?**

Path: Training → Quality ← Equipment

Quality is a **collider** and is in the conditioning set → **Opened**.

Answer: **No**, not d-separated.
:::
:::


## d-Separation Decision Procedure

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;"><span class="step-number">1</span><br>List all paths<br>between X and Y</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;"><span class="step-number green">2</span><br>For each path, identify<br>every triplet structure</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;"><span class="step-number orange">3</span><br>Check if any triplet<br>blocks the path</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;"><span class="step-number teal">4</span><br>If ALL paths blocked:<br>d-separated</div>
</div>

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**A path is blocked if it contains at least one:**

- Chain or fork with middle node **in** the conditioning set, OR
- Collider with middle node (and all its descendants) **not in** the conditioning set

**d-Separation holds only if EVERY path is blocked.**
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Why this matters:** d-Separation tells us which conditional independencies our causal model **implies**. These are testable predictions — if the data violates them, our model is wrong.
:::


# MNH Application: Full Qualitative DAG {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Building the MNH program's causal model</span>


## Identifying the Variables

Let's build a comprehensive qualitative DAG for the MNH program's theory of change with ~10 key nodes:

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
**Strategic Options (Rectangles)**

- **Budget Allocation** {People, Products, Systems, Balanced}

**Mediating Variables (Ovals)**

- **Workforce** {Adequate, Low}
- **Training** {Completed, Not}
- **Equipment** {Available, Unavailable}
- **Referral System** {Functional, Weak}
- **Quality of Care** {Good, Poor}
- **Coverage** {High, Low}
:::

::: {.col}
**Contextual Variables (Ovals)**

- **Baseline Burden** {Severe, Moderate, Low}

**Objectives (Hexagons)**

- **NMR** {High >25/1000, Low <=25/1000}
- **MMR** {High, Low}
:::
:::


## The Full DAG Structure

<table style="width: 95%; margin: 0.2em auto; font-size: 0.45em;">
<tr>
<td colspan="7" style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder" style="padding: 0.3em 0.6em;">Baseline Burden</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr><td colspan="7" style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.2em; color: #F8A623;">↓ [+]&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;↓ [+]</td></tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect" style="padding: 0.3em 0.6em;">Budget<br>Allocation</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">→ [+]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.3em 0.5em; margin-bottom: 0.2em;">Workforce</div><br>
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.3em 0.5em; margin-bottom: 0.2em;">Equipment</div><br>
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.3em 0.5em;">Referral Sys</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">→ [+]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.3em 0.6em;">Quality<br>of Care</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow negative">→ [-]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome hexagon" style="padding: 0.3em 0.6em; border-style: double;">NMR</div><br><br>
<div class="dag-node outcome hexagon" style="padding: 0.3em 0.6em; border-style: double;">MMR</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">↘ [+]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="padding: 0.3em 0.5em;">Training</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">→ [+]</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; font-size: 1em; color: #83BD00;">↑</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow positive">↑ [+]</span></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.3em 0.6em;">Coverage</div>
</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>


## Assigning Signs to Every Link

| Link | Sign | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Budget → Workforce | [+] | More funding enables hiring and retention |
| Budget → Equipment | [+] | More funding procures CPAP, PPH bundles, ultrasound |
| Budget → Referral System | [+] | More funding builds transport and data systems |
| Budget → Training | [+] | More funding enables BEmONC/CEmONC training |
| Training → Quality | [+] | Trained staff deliver higher quality care |
| Workforce → Quality | [+] | Adequate staffing prevents burnout, enables protocols |
| Equipment → Quality | [+] | Available tools enable evidence-based interventions |
| Referral System → Coverage | [+] | Functional referrals increase service utilization |
| Coverage → Quality | [+] | Higher coverage enables systematic quality improvement |
| Quality → NMR | [-] | Better care reduces neonatal mortality |
| Quality → MMR | [-] | Better care reduces maternal mortality |
| Baseline Burden → NMR | [+] | Higher burden raises mortality (direct effect) |
| Baseline Burden → MMR | [+] | Higher burden raises maternal mortality |


## Identifying Triplets Within the Model

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Serial (Chain) Examples**

- Budget → Training → Quality
- Referral System → Coverage → Quality
- Budget → Workforce → Quality → NMR

*Conditioning on the mediator blocks the path.*
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box}
**Diverging (Fork) Examples**

- Workforce ← Budget → Equipment
- NMR ← Quality → MMR
- Training ← Budget → Referral System

*Budget is a common cause. Conditioning on it blocks spurious correlations between its children.*
:::
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .red}
**Converging (Collider) Examples**

- Workforce → Quality ← Equipment
- Training → Quality ← Equipment (via Training → Quality)
- Coverage → Quality ← Workforce

*Quality is a collider. Conditioning on Quality creates a spurious association between Workforce and Equipment ("explaining away").*
:::


## Testable Implications

Our qualitative DAG implies specific **conditional independencies** that we can test against data:

. . .

::: {.incremental}
- **Training ⊥ Equipment | Budget** — given budget allocation, training and equipment decisions are independent (fork structure)
- **Workforce ⊥ Referral System | Budget** — same logic; both are children of budget
- **Training ⊥ NMR | Quality** — given quality of care, training has no additional direct effect on NMR (chain structure)
- **Workforce ⊥ Equipment | Quality** — conditioning on the collider *violates* this; they should be dependent!
:::

. . .

::: {.key-takeaway}
**If the data violates any implied independence, our model is wrong and must be revised.**

This is how qualitative models generate testable predictions — even without numbers.
:::


# R Code Workshop {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Building and analyzing the MNH DAG in R</span>


## R: Define the Full MNH DAG with dagitty

```{r}
#| label: mnh-dag-define
#| echo: true

library(dagitty)
library(ggdag)
library(ggplot2)

# Define the comprehensive MNH qualitative DAG
mnh_dag <- dagitty('dag {
  Budget          [pos="0,1"]
  Workforce       [pos="1.5,0"]
  Training        [pos="1.5,1"]
  Equipment       [pos="1.5,2"]
  Referral_System [pos="1.5,3"]
  Coverage        [pos="3,3"]
  Quality         [pos="3,1"]
  Baseline_Burden [pos="4,3"]
  NMR             [pos="4.5,0.5"]
  MMR             [pos="4.5,1.5"]

  Budget -> Workforce
  Budget -> Training
  Budget -> Equipment
  Budget -> Referral_System
  Training -> Quality
  Workforce -> Quality
  Equipment -> Quality
  Referral_System -> Coverage
  Coverage -> Quality
  Quality -> NMR
  Quality -> MMR
  Baseline_Burden -> NMR
  Baseline_Burden -> MMR
}')

# Verify it is a valid DAG (acyclic)
isAcyclic(mnh_dag)
```


## R: Visualize the DAG Color-Coded by Role

```{r}
#| label: mnh-dag-visualize
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 6

# Create a tidy dag object with exposure and outcome
tidy_dag <- tidy_dagitty(mnh_dag)

# Define node roles for coloring
node_roles <- data.frame(
  name = c("Budget", "Workforce", "Training", "Equipment",
           "Referral_System", "Coverage", "Quality",
           "Baseline_Burden", "NMR", "MMR"),
  role = c("Decision", "Mediator", "Mediator", "Mediator",
           "Mediator", "Mediator", "Mediator",
           "Context", "Outcome", "Outcome")
)

# Merge roles into the dag data
dag_data <- left_join(as.data.frame(tidy_dag), node_roles,
                      by = "name")

ggdag(mnh_dag) +
  theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "MNH Program: Qualitative Causal Model",
       subtitle = "10-node DAG for MNH investment decisions") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#005CB9", size = 16),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#666", size = 12))
```

::: {.callout-box}
**Color key:** Blue = Decision node, Green = Mediator, Amber = Context variable, Red = Outcome objective
:::


## R: Test d-Separation

```{r}
#| label: mnh-dsep-tests
#| echo: true

# Test 1: Is Training d-separated from NMR given Quality?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Training", "NMR", "Quality")
# Expected: TRUE (chain is blocked)

# Test 2: Is Workforce d-separated from Equipment? (unconditional)
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Workforce", "Equipment", c())
# Expected: FALSE (common parent Budget creates fork)

# Test 3: Is Workforce d-separated from Equipment given Budget?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Workforce", "Equipment", "Budget")
# Expected: TRUE (conditioning on common cause blocks fork)

# Test 4: Is Workforce d-separated from Equipment given Quality?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Workforce", "Equipment", "Quality")
# Expected: FALSE (conditioning on collider opens path!)

# Test 5: Is Training d-separated from Baseline_Burden?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Training", "Baseline_Burden", c())
# Expected: TRUE (no connecting path)
```

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Interpret each result:** TRUE means conditionally independent; FALSE means information flows between the two variables given the conditioning set.
:::


## R: Enumerate All Paths

```{r}
#| label: mnh-paths
#| echo: true

# Find all paths from Budget to NMR
budget_nmr_paths <- paths(mnh_dag,
                          from = "Budget",
                          to = "NMR")
print(budget_nmr_paths)

# Find all paths from Budget to MMR
budget_mmr_paths <- paths(mnh_dag,
                          from = "Budget",
                          to = "MMR")
print(budget_mmr_paths)

# Find paths between Workforce and Equipment (through collider)
workforce_equip_paths <- paths(mnh_dag,
                               from = "Workforce",
                               to = "Equipment")
print(workforce_equip_paths)
```

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Notice:** Budget → NMR has multiple directed paths (through Workforce, Training, Equipment, and Referral System). All carry a negative overall sign, reinforcing the investment logic.
:::


## R: Implied Conditional Independencies

```{r}
#| label: mnh-implied-ci
#| echo: true

# List all conditional independencies implied by the DAG
implied_cis <- impliedConditionalIndependencies(mnh_dag)
print(implied_cis)

# This tells us every testable prediction of our model.
# For example, if our model is correct, we should observe:
#   - Training _||_ Equipment | Budget
#   - Workforce _||_ Referral_System | Budget
#   - Training _||_ Baseline_Burden
#   - NMR _||_ MMR | Quality, Baseline_Burden
#
# If DHIS2 data violates any of these, we need to revise the DAG.

# Count total number of implied independencies
cat("Total implied conditional independencies:", length(implied_cis), "\n")
```

::: {.key-takeaway}
**The implied conditional independencies are the model's fingerprint.** Each one is a testable prediction. When we get DHIS2 data (Ch 10), we can check every one.
:::


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**Qualitative causal models are the foundation of rigorous analysis.**

A DAG encodes your causal beliefs explicitly using four node types and signed directed links.

The three triplet structures — **chain**, **fork**, and **collider** — govern all information flow. Chains and forks are blocked by conditioning; colliders are *opened* by conditioning.

**d-Separation** translates graph structure into testable conditional independence predictions.

For MNH programs: the qualitative DAG reveals that People, Products, and Systems investments are **complements** flowing through Quality of Care — and that conditioning on Quality creates collider bias between investment pillars.
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 3 — The Interview Case**

Putting qualitative models to work in a structured case study:

- Building a DAG from stakeholder interviews
- Translating qualitative beliefs into a causal diagram
- Identifying key assumptions and disagreements
- Preparing the model for quantification
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Then: Chapter 4 — Quantitative Causal Models**

Adding numbers to the structure:

- Conditional probability tables (replacing [+]/[-] with actual probabilities)
- Bayes' rule for updating beliefs with evidence
- Computing posterior probabilities in the full MNH network
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[Inference and Intervention | Chapter 2: Qualitative Causal Models]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
